title: The Nicene Creed, Part 2
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: The Nicene Creed __ Part 2 (Elder Nick Anderson).txt
outline: Part 2 of the Nicene Creed series
Exposition of the relevant article(s) of the Nicene Creed
summary: Part 2 of the October Sunday School series on the Nicene Creed, presented by Elder Nick Anderson. Continues the verse-by-verse exposition of the Creed in its historical and theological context.
transcript:
## We Believe: Why Doctrine Matters

The Nicene Creed begins with the words "We believe." We are not a people who just believe in general — like signs posted around a city reading simply *Believe* with no content. We believe *specific things*, and we are able to articulate what those things are. What we believe about Christ, about the Trinity, about sin and salvation, has eternal consequences.

Today we examine why doctrine matters, what the state of theology in our churches looks like, and what we must do about it.

## The New Testament Commands Regarding Doctrine

Paul's instructions to Timothy are unambiguous. In 1 Timothy 1:3–4 he charges Timothy to ensure that no other doctrine is taught. In 1 Timothy 4:1 he warns that in later times some will depart from the faith, giving heed to deceiving spirits and doctrines of demons. A few verses later:

> *"If you instruct the brethren in these things, you will be a good minister of Jesus Christ, nourished in the words of faith and of the good doctrine which you have carefully followed."* (1 Timothy 4:6)

> *"Take heed to yourself and to the doctrine. Continue in them, for in doing this you will save both yourself and those who hear you."* (1 Timothy 4:16)

In 1 Timothy 6:3–5, Paul warns that anyone who teaches otherwise and does not consent to wholesome words has a corrupt mind and is destitute of the truth. His instruction: "From such withdraw yourself." In 2 Timothy 1:13: "Hold fast the pattern of sound words which you have heard from me, in faith and love which are in Christ Jesus." In Titus 1:9, an elder must be holding fast the faithful word so that he can both exhort and convict those who contradict.

The Old Testament commanded it too. The Shema — "Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one" — was to be taught to children, talked about in the home, written on doorposts (Deuteronomy 6:4–9). Psalm 78 describes the passing of God's truth from generation to generation as a sacred duty.

The early church was marked by it. Acts 2:42 says the first believers "continued steadfastly in the apostles' doctrine and fellowship, in the breaking of bread, and in prayers." Even as their lives were at risk from persecution, they were devoted to doctrine. Believing rightly and confessing the faith is necessary for salvation (Romans 10:9–10).

## The State of Theology Today

Ligonier Ministries conducts a State of Theology survey roughly every three years. The results are not encouraging. Among those who identify as Christians in the United States:

- **64%** agreed that everyone is born innocent in the eyes of God. This directly contradicts the biblical doctrine of original sin.
- **53%** agreed that most people are good by nature. Romans 3 says there is no one righteous — no, not one. Genesis 6:5 says every intent of the thoughts of man's heart was "only evil continually."
- **53%** agreed that the Holy Spirit is a force, not a personal being. That is heresy. The Holy Spirit is the third person of the Trinity — a person, not a force.
- **47%** agreed that God accepts the worship of all religions. Jesus said: "I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me" (John 14:6).
- **49%** agreed that Jesus was a great teacher but was not God. Forty-nine percent of professing Christians just placed themselves outside of Christianity. If Jesus is not God, you are a heretic. Period.
- **71%** disagreed that even the smallest sin deserves eternal damnation — showing a profound misunderstanding of both the holiness of God and the gravity of sin.
- **100%** of self-identified evangelicals agreed that "the Bible is the highest authority for what I believe." And yet the rest of the survey shows that this stated commitment has almost no practical effect on what they actually believe.

Notice which doctrines the survey reveals are most widely denied: the deity of Christ, the deity of the Holy Spirit, and consequently the doctrine of the Trinity. These are the exact doctrines the Nicene Creed was written to defend. The creed remains as necessary as it ever was.

## Six Things This Teaches Us

**First, the warnings of Scripture are there for very good reason.** We are not naturally good theologians. It takes only one generation of failing to teach the next generation for a culture to become completely ignorant of what Scripture teaches. That appears to be precisely what has happened.

**Second, we need people who are well-trained and able to teach.** Paul himself, after his conversion on the Damascus road, spent three years in Arabia studying before he began his public ministry. He then instructed Timothy to train faithful men who would be able to teach others (2 Timothy 2:2). We should not expect pastoral training to be easy. The pastoral calling has eternal consequences for the souls of congregations — consequences more serious than any profession, however demanding. We need young men willing to deny themselves, sacrifice comfort, work hard through rigorous training, and give their lives to shepherding the church.

**Third, we need to be people who are willing to learn.** If you have come to the point of thinking you no longer need to be taught anything, you have been puffed up with pride. We will never stop learning about God, because he is infinite and we are finite. Even in eternity we will be learning more about the Lord. When we hear the same truth preached again, Paul says it is "safe" for us — because we are so prone to wander (Philippians 3:1).

**Fourth, we need historical awareness.** The church has fought every major theological battle before. There is nothing new under the sun. Knowing church history equips us to recognize old heresies in new clothing and respond with the wisdom of those who have gone before us.

**Fifth, we need to recover the life of the mind.** G. K. Chesterton observed that what infects the modern world is a "swollen pride in the possession of modern thought combined with a comparative neglect of thought." Short-form content — social media posts, YouTube Shorts, TikTok videos — cultivates superficiality. Nothing means anything for long. Christians are commanded to meditate on God's Word (Psalm 1), which means thinking deeply, turning a text over and examining it from different angles, not scrolling past it in two seconds. We are to love the Lord with all our *mind*.

**Sixth, love the Bible.** Read it, study it, meditate on it, memorize it. Be able to tell the story of Scripture from Genesis to Revelation. Know how the whole story points to Christ and to the salvation God provides. The best guard against unorthodox belief is knowing Scripture, cherishing it, and obeying it.

## Conclusion

We are not just a people who believe. We believe the Scriptures. We believe certain fundamental truths, and the Nicene Creed is there to remind us what those truths are. If you leave the creed, you have left the faith — not because the creed is a final authority, but because the creed accurately expresses what the universal church has concluded from Scripture about the most basic truths of Christianity. Let us be diligent to know these things, teach them, guard them, and pass them on.
